title: Impairment of hippocampal long-term potentiation by Alzheimer amyloid ?-peptides
abstract:
Impairment of Hippocampal Long-Term
Potentiation by Alzheimer Amyloid
b-Peptides
Qi-Sheng Chen, Bruce L. Kagan, Yutaka Hirakura, and Cui-Wei Xie*
Department of Psychiatry and Biobehavioral Sciences, Neuropsychiatric Institute, University of California-Los
Angeles, Los Angeles
Although it is generally believed that amyloid b (Ab) pep-
tides contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease,
the precise role of these peptides in the development of
memory loss of Alzheimer’s disease, has not been fully
understood. The present study examined the effect of sev-
eral synthetic Ab peptides on long-term potentiation (LTP),
a cellular model of learning and memory, in rat hippocampal
slices. Brief perfusion of slices with low concentrations (200
nM or 1 mM) of Ab1–42, Ab1–40 or their active fragment
Ab25-–35 signiﬁcantly inhibited LTP induction without affect-
ing the basal synaptic transmission and posttetanic poten-
tiation in the dentate medial perforant path. A similar effect
of Ab25–35 was also observed in the Schaffer colleteral-CA1
pathway. When comparing actions of several Ab variants
derived from Ab25–35, the N-terminal sequence of Ab25–35
was found necessary for inhibiting LTP. In addition, Ab
variants lacking neurotoxic action and aggregating property
were also able to block LTP, suggesting that this effect was
neurotoxicity independent. Our ﬁndings demonstrated that
subneurotoxic
concentrations
of
Ab
peptides
could
strongly suppress long-term synaptic plasticity in the hip-
pocampus. Such an effect might underlie the memory def-
icits seen in Alzheimer’s disease before neuronal cell loss.
J. Neurosci. Res. 60:65–72, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: amyloid b-peptide; hippocampus; long-term
potentiation; dentate gyrus; CA1; Alzheimer’s disease
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is the most frequent cause
of dementia in the elderly, characterized in part by pro-
gressive loss of memory and accumulation of amyloid b
(Ab) peptides in the brain. Ab peptides are 39–42 amino
acid peptide produced as soluble metabolic products of the
amyloid precursor protein (APP). In the AD brain, Ab
aggregates to form insoluble amyloid ﬁbrils in senile
plaques and cerebrovasculature. Extensive in vitro studies
have ascribed many neurotoxic effects to the aggregated
form of Ab (Mattson, 1997), with the effects being me-
diated by reactive oxygen species (Behl et al., 1994),
elevation of [Ca21]i (Mattson et al., 1992) or induction of
neuron killing by reactive microglia (Meda et al., 1995).
Although these ﬁndings have implicated Ab in the mech-
anism of neuronal degeneration in the AD brain, it re-
mains to be clariﬁed how these actions of Ab can be linked
to dementia, the primary clinical manifestation of AD.
Recent studies have shown that direct injections of
synthetic Ab into the brain (Nitta et al., 1997; Sweeney et
al., 1997) or overexpression of Ab-generating fragments of
APP (Moran et al., 1995; Hsiao et al., 1996; Nalbantoglu
et al., 1997; Chapman et al., 1999) cause learning and
memory deﬁcits in various animal models. These ﬁndings
have strengthened the connection between Ab accumu-
lation in the brain and development of cognitive deﬁcits.
Interestingly, memory deﬁcits induced by overexpression
of APP variants are not always associated with signiﬁcant
neuronal cell death in the brain (Nalbantoglu et al., 1997;
Chapman et al., 1999). Thus, at early stages of Ab accu-
mulation the peptide may affect learning and memory via
relatively subtle mechanisms involving dysfunction of se-
lected neurons or synapses rather than massive neuronal
degeneration. Hippocampal long-term potentiation (LTP)
may provide a convenient model system to investigate
such mechanisms.
LTP, a long-lasting enhancement of synaptic efﬁcacy
after brief, high-frequency afferent stimulation, has been
widely used as a model of activity-dependent synaptic
plasticity, and is thought to underlie learning and memory.
Gene targeting and transgenic studies have shown in many
cases impairment of hippocampal LTP associated with
learning and memory deﬁcits following altered gene ex-
pression of selected protein molecules (Bach et al., 1995;
Molinari et al., 1996; Son et al., 1996). In view of this
close association, hippocampal LTP seems a suitable model
for studying molecular and cellular mechanisms of demen-
tia associated with various neurological diseases. Indeed,
impaired hippocampal LTP has been observed after over-
Contract grant sponsor: National Health Institutes; Contract grant numbers:
DA08571, DA05010, MH01174; Contract grant sponsor: UCLA Alzhei-
mer’s Disease Center; Contract grant sponsor: UCLA AIDS Institute;
Contract grant sponsor: UCLA Stein-Oppenheimer Fund.
*Correspondence to: Dr. Cui-Wei Xie, Department of Psychiatry and
Biobehavioral Sciences, UCLA-NPI, 760 Westwood Plaza, Los Angeles,
CA 90024-1759. E-mail: xiexy@ucla.edu
Received 20 August 1999; Revised 6 December 1999; Accepted 9 De-
cember 1999
Journal of Neuroscience Research 60:65–72 (2000)
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 expression of the carboxy terminus of APP (Nalbantoglu
et al., 1997) or a mutated human APP (APP695SWE)
(Chapman et al., 1999). LTP impairments in these mice
are accompanied by spatial learning deﬁcits and in some
cases by minimal or no loss of presynaptic or postsynaptic
elementary structural elements in the hippocampus (Chap-
man et al., 1999). These ﬁndings support a causative role
of abnormal processing of APP in hippocampal dysfunc-
tion, and indicate that hippocampal LTP is a sensitive test
for detecting the early deterioration of synaptic function
before development of evident structural alterations. It is,
however, not directly tested in the above studies whether
the observed LTP deﬁcits are caused by generation and
accumulation of Ab or other proteolytic fragments of
APP, such as the C-terminal fragments C-100. Two pre-
vious studies have directly tested effect of Ab1–42 or
Ab1–40 on hippocampal LTP in rats, but yielded contro-
versial results, with Ab either blocking (Cullen et al.,
1997) or increasing (Wu et al., 1995) LTP in different
hippocampal subregions. To further clarify the effect of
Ab on hippocampal synaptic plasticity, the present study
examined the acute effect of several Ab peptides and their
variants on LTP induction in the dentate gyrus and area
CA1 of rat hippocampal slices.
METHODS
Slice Preparation
Male Sprague–Dawley rats (25–45 days old) were anes-
thetized with halothane and decapitated. The brain was cooled
in 4°C artiﬁcial cerebrospinal ﬂuid (ACSF), that contains (in
mM): NaCl, 120; NaHCO3, 25; KCl, 3.3; NaH2PO4, 1.23;
CaCl2, 1.8; MgSO4, 1.2 and D-glucose, 10 at pH 7.4. A vibro-
slicer (Campden) was used to cut whole brain into coronal slices
of 500 mm. The slices were further dissected into hemispheric
ones and placed into a holding chamber in ACSF bubbled
continuously with 95% O2/5% CO2.
Extracellular Recordings
After at least 1 hr equilibration in the holding chamber,
the slice was transferred into a submerged recording chamber
and perfused at a rate of 2-–3 ml/min with warmed ACSF (30 6
1°C). Synaptic responses were evoked by constant stimulus
pulses (0.1 ms, 0.06–0.4 mA) delivered through sharpened
monopolar tungsten electrodes, and recorded extracellularly
with glass micropipettes ﬁlled with 2 M NaCl. In the dentate
gyrus, the medial perforant path was stimulated in the middle
third of the molecular layer, and evoked responses were re-
corded from the granule cell body layer and dendritic layer
(dentate molecular layer). In the CA1 region, Schaffer collateral-
evoked responses were recorded from the pyramidal cell body
layer and dendritic layer (stratum radiatum). Field excitatory
postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs), that reﬂect depolarizing currents
ﬂowing through dendrites of postsynaptic neurons, were re-
corded from dendritic layers in all experiments and used as the
major index of synaptic strength. In some experiments popula-
tion spikes were also recorded from the cell body layer, that was
proportional to the number of neurons ﬁring action potentials
during synaptic activity.
LTP Experiments
LTP was induced by a single high-frequency stimulus
train (100 Hz, 1 sec) to the medial perforant path or Schaffer
collaterals. At the beginning of each experiment, test pulses that
evoke 40–50% of the maximum EPSP slope in normal ACFS
were applied once every min for baseline collection. The stim-
ulus train was then applied either in control medium, or in the
presence of Ab peptides after a 20-min peptide perfusion. The
peptide, if applied, was washed away immediately after the train,
and the same test pulses were resumed to examine the changes
in evoked responses in peptide-free perfusion medium. The
amount of LTP was quantiﬁed 30 min after the train, and was
expressed as percent increases in the slope of ﬁeld EPSPs or in
the amplitude of population spikes relative to the baseline levels.
An increase of $20% from the baseline was considered as a
signiﬁcant LTP.
Drug Application
Ab25–35 was purchased from BACHEM (Torrance, CA).
Ab1–40 and Ab1-42 were from the Keck Peptide Synthesis
Laboratory at Yale University. Ab25–35 variants were gifts from
Dr. Carl Cotman of University of California-Irvine. All peptides
were prepared as 1 mM stock solution in H2O, stored in small
aliquots at 220°C, and diluted to desired ﬁnal concentrations in
ACSF immediately before application in each experiment. The
peptide solutions were directly added into the perfusion medium
via a three-way tap for bath application. The dead time of the
perfusion system was about one min. Samples of applied peptide
solutions were taken during the experiments and visually in-
spected under a phase-contrast microscope (400 3) for the
presence of ﬁbrillar or sheetlike aggregates as described (Pike et
al., 1995). No evident aggregates were observed in samples
inspected.
Data Analysis
Recorded signals were digitized and stored on computer
disks for off-line analysis. The group data were expressed as
mean 6 standard error (SE) and subjected to a one-way analysis
of variance (ANOVA) to test for overall statistical signiﬁcance.
Comparisons between groups were made by Student’s t-tests.
Statistical signiﬁcance was deﬁned as P , 0.05.
RESULTS
Effect of Ab on Basal Synaptic Transmission and
Posttetanic Potentiation
The initial experiments examined whether Ab pep-
tides could affect the basal synaptic transmission in the
hippocampal dentate gyrus. The input-output relationship
during medial perforant path stimulation, plotted as the
slope of ﬁled EPSP versus the amplitude of presynaptic
ﬁber volley, was determined in the control medium and
during Ab perfusion from the same slices. Applications of
Ab1–42 or Ab25–35 at 200 nM caused no evident changes
in this input-output coupling (Fig.1A,C). The mean ratios
of ﬁeld EPSP slopes (2mV/ms) to presynaptic ﬁber volley
sizes (mV), determined at 50% maximal slope level, were
not signiﬁcantly different between those measured in Ab
peptides and in control medium: 21.65 6 0.26 ms21
66
Chen et al.

Effect of Ab on Basal Synaptic Transmission and
The initial experiments examined whether Ab pep-
tides could affect the basal synaptic transmission in the
hippocampal dentate gyrus. The input-output relationship
during medial perforant path stimulation, plotted as the
slope of ﬁled EPSP versus the amplitude of presynaptic
ﬁber volley, was determined in the control medium and
during Ab perfusion from the same slices. Applications of
Ab1–42 or Ab25–35 at 200 nM caused no evident changes
in this input-output coupling (Fig.1A,C). The mean ratios
of ﬁeld EPSP slopes (2mV/ms) to presynaptic ﬁber volley
sizes (mV), determined at 50% maximal slope level, were
not signiﬁcantly different between those measured in Ab
peptides and in control medium: 21.65 6 0.26 ms21
during Ab1–42 perfusion versus 21.64 6 0.20 ms21 in
control medium (n 5 5, P 5 0.97 ); 21.65 6 0.14 ms21
during Ab25–35 perfusion versus 21.76 6 0.18 ms21 for
the controls (n 5 5, P 5 0.66).
We next examined the posttetanic potentiation
(PTP), a form of short-term synaptic plasticity, in Ab-
treated slices. Slices were perfused with the NMDA re-
D-2-amino-5-phosphonovaleric acid
Fig. 1. Ab1–42 and Ab25–35 induce no signiﬁcant changes in the basal
synaptic transmission and posttetanic potentiation (PTP) in the dentate
medial perforant path. Left panels: the scatter plots of ﬁeld EPSP slopes
versus presynaptic ﬁber volleys (input–output plots) obtained in ACSF
(Control) and during Ab perfusion from the same slices. After the ﬁrst
input–output test in ACSF, the slices were perfused with Ab1–42
(A, n 5 5) or Ab25–35 (C, n 5 5) for approximately 35 min to complete
the second test that started after 20 min perfusion with Ab peptides.
Note that Ab peptides induced no marked changes in data distribution.
Right panels: representative experiments showing PTP induced by a
single high frequency stimulus (HFS) at 100 Hz for a duration of 1 sec.
Slices treated with 200 nM Ab1–42 (B) or Ab25–35 (D) displayed similar
degree of potentiation in EPSP slopes immediately after the train as
compared with control slices. All slices were treated with D-APV (50
mM,10 min) before and during the HFS to prevent long-term poten-
Amyloid b-Peptides Inhibit Hippocampal LTP
(D-APV) at 50 mM for 10 min. A high frequency stimu-
lation (100 Hz, 1s) applied in the presence of D-APV was
unable to induce LTP but elicited a transient enhancement
of ﬁeld EPSP slope immediately after tetanization. Such an
enhancement was not altered by Ab1–42 or Ab25–35 (Fig.
1B,D). At one min posttrain, the PTP was 117 6 3% in
Ab1–42-treated slices (n 5 6) and 117 6 2% in Ab25–35-
treated slices (n 5 6), neither being signiﬁcantly different
from the PTP in control slices (122 6 2%, n 5 6, P . 0.7
Inhibition of LTP by Ab1-42 and Ab1–40
Ab, although not affecting the basal synaptic func-
tion and PTP, strongly inhibited the induction of LTP in
the dentate gyrus. As shown in Figure 2, a single high
frequency stimulus train to the medial perforant path of
control slices induced signiﬁcant increases in the slope of
ﬁeld EPSPs and in the amplitude of population spikes, that
lasted for at least 30 min. The same stimulus train, how-
ever, failed to induce any long-lasting changes in synaptic
responses in slices perfused with 200 nM Ab1–42. In
Ab1–42-treated slices (n 5 7), 30 min after the train the
ﬁeld EPSP slope and population spikes were 101 6 1%
and 104 6 5% of the baseline, respectively, both being
signiﬁcantly lower than the corresponding values of the
control group (137 6 4% and 167 6 6%, n 5 8, P , 0.01
for both comparisons). Application of Ab1–40 at 200 nM
also completely inhibited LTP of EPSP slope (n 5 5,
100 6 1%, P , 0.01), but only slightly suppressed LTP of
population spikes (139 6 20%, P . 0.05) (Fig. 2B).
To rule out the possibility that Ab inhibited LTP
indirectly by changing GABAA-mediated synaptic inhibi-
tion, the effect of Ab1–42 on dentate LTP was also tested
in the presence of a GABAA receptor blocker bicuculline
methiodide (BMI, 50 mM). Slices were perfused with
BMI throughout the experiment. Ab1–42 (200 nM) was
added into the perfusion medium after the baseline col-
lection and applied for 20 min. The high frequency stim-
ulation delivered in the presence of Ab and BMI produced
a LTP of EPSP slopes (111 6 7%, n 5 5, P , 0.05)
signiﬁcantly smaller than that in slices treated with BMI
only (144 6 13%, n 5 4). Thus, removal of GABAergic
transmission did not prevent Ab-induced LTP reduction.
Ab25–35, the active domain of Ab (Pike et al., 1995),
was tested in both dentate gyrus and area CA1 of hip-
pocampal slices. Similar to the full length Ab, Ab25–35
strongly suppressed induction of LTP in the dentate me-
dial perforant path (Fig. 3A,B). The effect of Ab25–35 was
dose-dependent, with LTP of EPSP slope unaltered at 20
nM (137 6 7%, n 5 5), but signiﬁcantly reduced at 200
nM (107 6 7%, n 5 8, P , 0.01) or 1 mM (97 6 4%, n 5
7, P , 0.01) relative to the control group (136 6 5%, n 5
5). Slices treated with Ab35–25, an analogous peptide of
Ab25–35 with reverse sequence, displayed normal LTP of
EPSPs (137 6 3%, n 5 6), suggesting the effect of Ab25–35
was sequence speciﬁc (Fig. 3B).
Ab25–35 also dramatically reduced LTP in the CA1
region induced by a high frequency stimulation to the
Schaffer collaterals (Fig. 3C). In slices treated with 200 nM
Ab25–35 (n 5 6), LTP of EPSP slope and population spikes
Fig. 2. Ab1–42 and Ab1–40 inhibit LTP in the dentate medial perforant
path. (A) Representative experiments showing induction of LTP and
its blockade by Ab1–42. The HFS (100 Hz, 1s) induced a signiﬁcant
potentiation of ﬁled EPSP in a control slice, but failed to do so in
another slice perfused with 200 nM Ab1–42. Application of Ab1–42 and
the HFS is indicated by the horizontal bar and arrow, respectively. The
numbers indicate corresponding time points for the sample ﬁeld EPSPs
shown at the top panel. Scale bars 5 1 mV/5 ms. (B) Group data for
the effect of Ab1–40 and Ab1–42 (200 nM, 20 min). The HFS was given
at the end of Ab perfusion as shown in 1A. The amount of LTP was
determined 30 min after the HFS in Ab-free perfusion medium, and
was expressed as percent increases of evoked responses from the baseline
levels. Control: n 5 5; Ab1–40: n 5 5, Ab1–42: n 5 7. **P , 0.01 as
compared to the control group.
was 105 6 4% and 110 6 8%, respectively, signiﬁcantly
lower than the corresponding values in the controls
(135 6 5% and 201 6 6%, n 5 5, P , 0.01 for both
Effect of Ab25–35 Variants on Dentate LTP
To explore the structure-functional relationship and
underlying mechanisms for the effect of Ab on LTP, the
action of several Ab25–35 variants was tested in the dentate.
The Ab25–35 variants were synthesized either by substitu-
tion of the C-terminal Met35 with other amino acids
(denoted as M35), or by deletion of two amino acids from
the N-terminal sequence (Ab27–35). As shown in Figure 4,
three out of four C-terminal variants blocked LTP to a
similar extent as Ab25–35 did. The LTP of EPSP slopes in
slices treated with these variants, M35Cys, M35Nle and
M35Val, was respectively 104 6 2% (n 5 6), 96 6 5%
( n 5 5) and 97 6 5% (n 5 5), all being signiﬁcantly lower
than LTP of the control group (136 6 5%, n 5 5, P ,
0.01). In contrast, the slices treated with the N-terminal
variant, Ab27–35, displayed a normal LTP (133 6 3%, n 5
5, P , 0.05), suggesting that the N-terminal sequence of
Ab25–35 was required for inhibition of LTP. Interestingly,
M35Nle and M35Val, two C-terminal variants that were
not cytotoxic in cultured hippocampal neurons (Pike et
al., 1995), completely blocked LTP. M35Nle was also
known to yield no aggregating peptide under “aged”
conditions (Pike et al., 1995). Thus, neither induction of
neurotoxicity nor the aggregation state of Ab peptides
seemed a necessary requirement for LTP inhibition under
Impairment of Hippocampal LTP by Ab: Possible
The hippocampus is a critical brain region in early
stages of memory formation (Kennedy and Marder, 1992).
Studies in primates and human patients have demonstrated
that hippocampal lesions produce severe anterograde am-
nesia, an inability to form new memories, that is similar to
memory deﬁcits seen in AD patients. Indeed, several lines
of evidence have indicated that degeneration or dysfunc-
tion of the hippocampus may play an important role in the
development of AD dementia. First, in the AD brain the
hippocampus is one of the focal points for pathological
changes, susceptible to heavy accumulation of Ab plaques,
formation of neuroﬁbrillary tangles, cell loss and neuritic
dystrophy (Van Hoesen and Hyman, 1990; McKee et al.,
1991; Mann, 1996). The extent of these changes often
correlates well with the degree of clinical dementia (Mc-
Kee et al., 1991; Mann, 1996). Secondly, exogenously
applied Ab has potent cytotoxic effects in cultured hip-
pocampal neurons (Loo et al., 1993; Ueda et al., 1997).
Accordingly, overexpression of Ab-generating APP frag-
ments induces age-dependent degeneration of neurons
and synapses in the hippocampus (Oster-Granite et al.,
1996). Finally, injection of synthetic Ab into rat hip-
pocampus causes impaired performance in behavioral tests

Amyloid b-Peptides Inhibit Hippocampal LTP
requiring short-term memory (Sweeney et al., 1997), that
is in line with our in vitro ﬁndings that Ab inhibits
Further strengthening the evidence for a causative
role of Ab in hippocampal dysfunction and memory def-
icits of AD, the present study has demonstrated that ap-
plication of Ab peptides strongly inhibits LTP induction
in two major hippocampal pathways: the dentate medial
perforant path and Schaffer colllateral-CA1 pathway. Our
results are complementary to a previous in vivo study
showing impaired maintenance of CA1 LTP in rats in-
jected with Ab1–40 or Ab1–42 (Cullen et al., 1997); and
also are consistent with recent in vitro studies using hip-
pocampal slices from APP transgenic mice (Nalbantoglu et
al., 1997; Chapman et al., 1999). An earlier brief study
reported that Ab1–40 enhanced LTP in the commissural/
associational pathway of rat dentate gyrus in brain slices
treated with the GABAA blocker picrotoxin (Wu et al.,
1995). In the present study, however, various Ab peptides
induced highly reproducible LTP impairments in the me-
dial perforant path of dentate gyrus, either in the presence
or absence of the GABAA blocker. Whether this discrep-
ancy reﬂects different LTP properties in these two dentate
pathways or was caused by other technical variants, such as
different stimulation paradigms, remains to be determined
The focus of the present study is the acute effect of
various Ab peptides in the dentate medial perforant path,
that has not been examined previously. The perforant
path—dentate granule cell synapse forms the ﬁrst link of
the basic trisynaptic pathway of hippocampal formation—
serving as a gate controlling the information ﬂow entering
this brain region. It is conceivable that Ab-induced func-
tional impairment at dentate synapses, combined with its
effect in area CA1, could signiﬁcantly disrupt information
processing and storage in the entire hippocampal circuitry.
Such a mechanism can be particularly relevant to the
development of AD dementia. Recent studies have shown
that the perforant path axonally transports and processes
APP, causing accumulation of potentially amyloidogenic
fragments at synaptic sites in the dentate (Buxbaum et al.,
APP717V-F, the human APP containing the familial AD
mutation, the animals develop brain region-speciﬁc Ab
depositions conﬁned mostly to the entorhinal cortex and
to the terminal region of the perforant path in the dentate
(Su and Ni, 1998). Similarly, in the human AD brain
Ab-containing neuritic plaques are found prominently in
the terminal zone of the perforant path, the outer two
thirds of the dentate molecular layer (Van Hoesen and
Hyman, 1990). Thus, the perforant path seems a particu-
larly vulnerable target to the early, selective deposition of
Ab in the AD brain, and loss of long-term synaptic plas-
ticity at perforant path synapses may be an early sign of
Neurotoxicity Is Not Required for Impairment of
One candidate mechanism for Ab-induced LTP im-
pairments is neuronal and synaptic degeneration resulted
from cytotoxic actions of the peptide. Induction of neu-
rotoxicity in cultured hippocampal or cortical neurons,
however, usually requires incubation with much higher
concentrations of Ab (10–50 mM) for an extended period
Fig. 4. The effect of Ab25–35 variants on dentate LTP. All peptides were applied at 200 nM for 20
min. The HFS (100 Hz, 1s) was applied at the end of peptide perfusion, and LTP was measured 30
min after the train. N 5 5–6 for each group. **P , 0.01 as compared to the control group.
of 16–48 hr (Pappolla et al., 1997; Ueda et al., 1997), and
formation of signiﬁcant peptide aggregation under “aged”
conditions is also a necessary condition (Pike et al., 1995).
In the present study LTP impairments were observed after
a brief perfusion of freshly prepared Ab peptides at con-
centrations as low as 200 nM. It is thus unlikely that
signiﬁcant neurotoxicity was produced and responsible for
LTP inhibition under our experimental conditions. Con-
sistent with a lack of neurotoxicity, Ab1–42 and Ab25–35
caused no signiﬁcant changes in the basal synaptic trans-
mission and in short-term synaptic plasticity. Furthermore,
inhibition of LTP by Ab25–35 variants was not correlated
with their neurotoxic actions. M35Nle and M35Val, two
variants that produced no cytotoxicity in cultured hip-
pocampal neurons (Pike et al., 1995), were found equally
potent as unmodiﬁed Ab peptides in blocking dentate
LTP. It is also known that removal or substitute of Met35,
the C-terminal amino acid of Ab25–35, prevented Ab-
induced neurotoxicity and oxidative stress (Pike et al.,
1995; Subramaniam et al., 1995). In our experiments,
however, the majority of C-terminal variants still retained
the ability to suppress LTP. Collectively, these results have
suggested that neurotoxicity is unlikely the major cause for
inhibition of LTP under our conditions.
Possible Mechanisms for Ab-Induced LTP
Previous (Brorson et al., 1995) and our recent Ca21
imaging studies (Wei et al., 1999) have shown that acute
applications of Ab peptides at the concentration effective
for LTP inhibition (1 mM) induce transient intracellular
Ca21 rises and spontaneous Ca21 oscillations in cultured
hippocampal neurons. Because Ca21 plays a central role in
LTP mechanisms, it is intriguing to speculate that Ab-
induced Ca21 transients may interfere with normal intra-
cellular Ca21 signaling and consequently impair LTP gen-
eration. For example, intracellular Ca21 transients may
activate calcineurin, a Ca21/calmodulin-depedent protein
phosphatase, which could in turn desensitize NMDA re-
ceptor channels (Tong et al., 1995) and thereby impair
LTP induction. This idea has been supported by our most
recent ﬁnding that pretreating hippocampal slices with
speciﬁc calcineurin inhibitors prevents impairment of the
late-phase LTP by Ab1–42 (Chen and Xie, 1999).
Whether a similar mechanism is responsible for suppres-
sion of the early-phase LTP by Ab, as observed in the
present study, is currently under investigation.
Growing evidence has indicated that Ab and related
peptides can alter cellular ionic activity either through
interactions with existing ion channels or by formation of
de novo ion channels (Fraser et al., 1997; Mattson, 1997).
Several Ab25–35 variants (M35Cys, M35Nle and M35Val)
that strongly inhibited LTP in the present study are also
found to form cation-selective and Ca21-permeable ion
channels in planar lipid bilayers. In contrast, M35Tyr, a
variant lacking channel forming activity, failed to suppress
LTP. Whether the channel forming property of Ab pep-
tides contributes to observed Ca21 transients and LTP
impairments in the rat hippocampus are interesting issues
The present study demonstrated that brief perfusion
of low concentrations of Ab peptides strongly inhibited
LTP induction in the dentate gyrus and CA1 region of rat
hippocampal slices. The LTP impairment was not accom-
panied by alterations in the basal synaptic transmission or
short-term synaptic plasticity. The structural-activity rela-
tionship of Ab peptides, tested with a number of Ab25–35
variants, demonstrated that the N-terminal but not the
C-terminal sequence of Ab25–35 was required for LTP
inhibition. In addition, Ab variants lacking cytotoxic ac-
tion and aggregating property were still effective in sup-
pressing LTP. Thus, Ab seems able to disrupt long-term
synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus via mechanisms
independent of neurotoxicity, which may form the cellu-
lar basis for memory loss at early stages of AD.
